title: LGBT+ Legal Equality Index
subtitle: The index captures to which extent LGBT+ people have the same rights as straight and cisgender people. It combines 15 individual policies, such as the legality of same-sex relationships, marriage, and gender marker changes. It ranges from 0 to 100 (most equal).
caption:
LGBT+ Legal Equality Index

The index captures to which extent LGBT+ people have the same rights as straight and cisgender people. It combines 15 individual policies, such as the legality of same-sex relationships, marriage, and gender marker changes. It ranges from 0 to 100 (most equal).

This choropleth world map displays the 2025 LGBT+ Legal Equality Index, where scores range from 0 to 100, with higher values indicating greater legal equality. The legend uses a color gradient from dark red (0) through orange and yellow to teal and purple (100). Countries in Western Europe, including Spain, France, and the United Kingdom, along with Canada, Australia, and New Zealand, appear in the darkest purple band, indicating scores near the top of the scale, likely above 90. The United States and Mexico sit in a teal shade, placing them in the 70 to 80 range. Much of South America shows variation: Argentina and Uruguay are in purple, while Brazil and Colombia are in teal or light green, suggesting mid-to-high scores. In contrast, large parts of Africa and the Middle East are shaded in dark red or deep orange, corresponding to the lowest bands of the index, below 30. Russia and China appear in orange, falling between 20 and 40. India and Indonesia are in yellow-green, around 50 to 60. Several countries, particularly in central Africa and parts of Asia, show no data, indicated by hatching. The visual pattern reveals high scores concentrated in Western Europe, North America, and Oceania, while low scores dominate across Africa, the Middle East, and parts of Asia.
